summary: DHH explores the idea that humans have an innate need for struggle, drawing on Orwell's observation that we can imagine hell but not heaven, and Dostoevsky's claim that people will reject rational bliss to preserve autonomy. He argues that modern society, having solved many existential problems, now generates invented struggles—from prolonged COVID culture wars to woke ideology and its conservative counter-reaction. DHH suggests that traditional religion may have served as a healthier containment system for this psychological need, channeling the yearning for struggle through predefined narratives rather than destructive cultural battles. He concludes by recommending readers engage with Orwell, Dostoevsky, and Sowell to navigate these tensions.
key_points: Humans have a deep psychological need for struggle and will invent one when real existential challenges recede
Modern culture wars—COVID debates, woke ideology, counter-reactions—are symptoms of manufactured struggle by spiritually malnourished populations
Traditional religion may have been a superior mechanism for channeling humanity's innate need for conflict and meaning
The collective wisdom embedded in tradition, culture, and law is difficult to articulate but structurally profound, and we're only now seeing consequences of its retreat
The rapid establishment of progressive orthodoxy has paradoxically made conservative traditionalism the new counterculture
money_quotes: While humans can imagine hell in minute detail, heaven escapes anything but the most fuzzy, vague descriptions.
This yearning for an identity-defining struggle is so great that humans will invent one if need be.

Bredding pathological attacks on the pillars of society, and even civilization itself, in the search for a struggle worthy of the self-actualization needs present in especially the over-educated, spiritually-malnourished part of the populace.
We were broadly better off when the big, existential struggles were derived mostly from religious roleplaying.
Without a broad religious operating system, we end up chasing the same bugs in our innate psyche, but the modern fixes are often way worse.

The collective wisdom of the ages, as passed down to us through tradition, culture, and law, is often very hard to reason specifically about after the fact. But that doesn't mean the wisdom isn't present.
Being transgressive is now defined as the act of wearing a cross and attending latin mass.
key_insight: Humanity's hardwired need for struggle, once channeled through religion, now manifests as destructive culture wars because modern secular society offers no healthy substitute.
